title: Humility
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro

outline.title: Introduction: The Common Thread of Luke 14
subpoints: Last pass through Luke before returning to 1 Peter; the entire chapter — Sabbath healing, two feast parables, cost of discipleship — is held together by the theme of humility
Jesus is invited to a Sabbath dinner by a ruler of the Pharisees (likely a Sanhedrin member); the invitation was a trap: they watched him closely (v.1)
The chapter ends with the exhortation: 'He who has ears to hear, let him hear' — the contrast with Luke 15 is instructive: tax collectors and sinners came to hear Jesus while the Pharisees refused

outline.title: 1. The Sabbath Healing: Pride Confronted (vv.1-6)
subpoints: A man with dropsy (fluid retention, caused by liver disease from recurring malaria attacks) was likely brought by the Pharisees as bait; Jesus healed him by hugging him — taking the deformed, despised man into his arms
Jesus asks the lawyers and Pharisees: 'Is it lawful to heal on the Sabbath?' — they stay silent because any answer exposes them: 'no' shows their law supersedes God's; 'yes' deflates their trap
The follow-up question — 'which of you, if his donkey falls in a pit, would not pull it out on the Sabbath?' — uses their own logic against them; silence again
The Pharisees, by replacing God's law with their own rules, put themselves in the place of God — the height of pride; we often care more about offenses against us than offenses against God

outline.title: 2. Parable of the Wedding Feast (vv.7-14)
subpoints: Guests rush to the best seats (the most honored position at the bend of the U-shaped table arrangement); Jesus teaches: take the lowest seat and let the host move you up, rather than be moved down in shame

Verse 11: 'For whoever exalts himself will be humbled, and he who humbles himself will be exalted' — Jesus uses this principle at least three times; parallels Luke 18 (Pharisee boasts; tax collector beats his chest: 'be merciful to me, a sinner')
To the host (vv.12-14): don't invite those who can repay you (friends, family, rich neighbors); invite the poor, the maimed, the lame, the blind — you will be repaid at the resurrection of the just
Inviting those who cannot repay is not what earns heaven; it is the sign of a humble Christian whose eyes are on the heavenly reward, not earthly return

outline.title: 3. Parable of the Great Feast (vv.15-24)
subpoints: A guest blurts out 'Blessed is he who shall eat bread in the kingdom of God!' — probably trying to defuse the tension; Jesus's response implies the man doesn't understand what he is saying

Custom: guests RSVPed 'yes,' then a servant went again to summon them when all was ready; all who said yes now make lame excuses (buying a field without inspecting it; testing oxen already purchased; marriage didn't excuse social obligations)
The first guests = Jewish leaders who rejected their Messiah; the Master is angry — God does not take lightly the rejection of his free gospel offer
The poor, maimed, lame, blind, and those on the highways and hedges = us; we are the lame and blind; compelled to come by the persuasion of the gospel, not by force
The Elijah syndrome: don't think you are alone; God told Elijah to stop moping and do the work — and there are still 7,000 who have not bowed to Baal; the harvest will be an uncountable multitude

outline.title: 4. The Cost of Discipleship (vv.25-35)
subpoints: Jesus requires only one thing: total and complete surrender; 'hate' father, mother, wife, children, brothers, sisters, and his own life (v.26) — not literal hatred but: from the world's perspective, your loyalty to Christ looks like hatred
The cross is a symbol of death, not merely suffering (v.27); to carry your cross is not 'I will suffer this for Christ' but 'I am dying to self — Christ is increasing, I am decreasing'

Count the cost first (tower builder; king going to war against a superior force): Jesus does not want anyone deceived into following him; discipleship means you no longer exist — you are a new creature, completely dependent on him
Verse 33: 'Whoever of you does not forsake all that he has cannot be my disciple'; salt that loses its flavor is fit neither for land nor dung hill — useless; 'he who has ears to hear, let him hear'

summary: A sermon on the entirety of Luke 14 — a Sabbath dinner at the house of a leading Pharisee, two feast parables (wedding feast, great supper), and a section on the cost of discipleship — all held together by the theme of humility. The Pharisees are the epitome of pride in Jesus's day, having replaced God's law with their own rules and put themselves in God's place. Jesus heals a man with dropsy by hugging him — taking the despised, deformed man into his arms — and silences the Pharisees twice with rhetorical questions they dare not answer. The wedding feast parable teaches: don't rush to the best seat; take the lowest and let the host move you up. The dinner instruction flips social convention: invite those who cannot repay you, not those who will. The great feast parable draws the contrast between the first-invited guests (Jewish leaders who rejected the Messiah) and those from the streets and hedges (the poor, lame, and blind — us, the Gentiles). The Elijah syndrome (thinking yourself alone) is addressed: 7,000 had not bowed to Baal; the gospel harvest will be uncountable. The final section names the cost of discipleship: Jesus requires total and complete surrender — 'hate' your family (the world reads your loyalty to Christ as hatred) and carry your cross (symbol of death, not suffering). Count the cost first: discipleship means you die to self and become completely dependent on Christ.
transcript:
## Introduction

We are returning to 1 Peter in two weeks. As we consider the situation of one who was formerly a brother, it would do us good to consider our own faith and obedience before the Lord and where we stand with him.

Luke chapter 14. I'm going to read the whole chapter, and as we read through it I want you to look for what holds all the different stories together. What is the common thread?

Verse 1 through the end: a dinner on the Sabbath; two parables about feasts; and a section about the cost of following Jesus. If you saw the common thread — it is the idea of humility.

Now it is much easier to preach about other people's sins. And to talk about humility, you have to talk about pride. Pride is very present in my own life as a follower of Jesus Christ — something I continue repenting of. So we are going to talk about my sins this morning.

The chapter ends with an exhortation: *He who has ears to hear, let him hear.* Come to this chapter with open ears. It is interesting that in the beginning of chapter 15, tax collectors and sinners came to hear what Jesus had to say, while the Pharisees refused to hear. Brothers and sisters — sinners — let us listen.

## 1. The Setting: Dinner at a Pharisee's House (vv.1-6)

It is a Sabbath dinner. A ruler of the Pharisees — likely a member of the Sanhedrin — invited Jesus over after the synagogue service. What seemed like a nice gesture was actually a trap: *they watched him closely* (v.1). The Pharisees often tried to catch Jesus breaking one of their Sabbath rules — rules not in the Bible, but rules they had made that became more important to them than God's own law.

The man with dropsy in verse 2 — suffering from fluid retention caused by liver disease — was probably brought there by the Pharisees as bait.

Jesus asked them: *Is it lawful to heal on the Sabbath?* They stayed silent. Any answer exposed them. If they said no, they were admitting that their rules took precedence over God's law. If they said yes, the trap was sprung on themselves.

Since they would not answer, Jesus healed the man. And here — it is easy to miss in the English — it says he *took him.* That means he took him into his arms. Jesus hugged the deformed man, the one everyone despised, the one too ugly and too broken for anyone to care about. Even if Jesus had not healed him physically, the effect of being touched with compassion and affection by the creator of the universe would have made that man's day. Jesus — the perfect, holy Son of God — humbles himself to restore what is broken.

Then the follow-up question: *Which of you, having a donkey or ox fall into a pit on the Sabbath, would not immediately pull him out?* They could not answer. They take care of their animals on the Lord's Day — but not a suffering human being? They were more upset about their rules being broken than about God being glorified. They were more concerned about sins against themselves than sins against God.

## 2. The Wedding Feast Parable (vv.7-14)

They move inside for the meal. At a Jewish dinner, the table was arranged in a U-shape, with the most important seat at the top of the U. Jesus watched as everyone jostled to sit as close to the host as possible.

He told them: when you are invited to a wedding feast, do not take the best place — someone more important may have been invited, and the host will come to you and say, *give place to this man,* and you will have to walk in shame all the way to the last seat. Instead, take the lowest place. Let the host invite you up higher. Then you will have honor in front of everyone.

Jesus is not teaching Jewish wedding etiquette. He is illustrating what it means to be part of his kingdom: *For whoever exalts himself will be humbled, and he who humbles himself will be exalted* (v.11).

The same picture appears in Luke 18: the Pharisee stands and boasts — *Lord, I thank you that I am not like this tax collector; I do all these great things.* The tax collector can barely look up and just beats his chest: *Be merciful to me, a sinner.* Jesus says: it is the tax collector who goes home justified.

Pride is not consistent with the follower of Jesus Christ. Those who are alive to self will be prideful. And pride must be killed.

Then Jesus turned to the host (vv.12-14): when you give a dinner, do not invite your friends, your brothers, your relatives, or your rich neighbors — because they will invite you back and you will be repaid. Invite the poor, the maimed, the lame, the blind. They cannot repay you. You will be repaid at the resurrection of the just.

God calls us not to be respecters of persons. We all know which people we do not want to waste time on, and which ones we think are worth investing in. Jesus says: get rid of that thinking. Invest in people from whom you can get the least return — because that shows humility. It shows you understand what it means to follow Jesus Christ.

## 3. The Parable of the Great Feast (vv.15-24)

One of the guests, perhaps noticing the tension at the table, blurts out: *Blessed is he who shall eat bread in the kingdom of God!* It was probably an attempt to defuse the awkward situation by saying something he hoped would please Jesus. But Jesus's response implies he had no real idea what he was saying.

Jesus answered with the parable of the great feast. The custom was to invite guests twice: first an advance invitation (they all said yes, they are coming), then a second summons when the feast was ready. When the servant went out with the second invitation, everyone had lame excuses. I bought land and must inspect it — but no one buys land without inspecting it first, and if you did, it will be there tomorrow. I bought oxen and need to test them — but if you bought oxen without testing them first, it is too late now anyway. I just got married — but marriage did not excuse a person from social obligations under Jewish custom.

These are just excuses. The first group of guests represents the Jewish people, represented by their leaders — they had the invitation of God's word in the Old Testament, they had the prophets, they had the promises; and they were too proud to accept a Messiah who came to suffer for their sins.

The Master was angry. God does not take lightly the rejection of his free offer of the gospel. He will not take second place.

So what does the master do? He sends the servant to the streets and lanes — the poor, the maimed, the lame, the blind. And then to the highways and hedges. Who are these people? Who are the lame, the blind, the outcast on the hedges? It is us. That is you. That is me. We are the lame. We are the blind. We tend to think of ourselves as the exalted ones — but if we are in Christ, it is because we were the ones not initially invited who have now been summoned to the party.

The servant *compels* them to come (v.23) — not by force (one servant cannot compel a multitude) but by the persuasion of the gospel. The master wants his house full: a multitude too great to number.

Do not get the Elijah syndrome. Elijah had enormous success — defeated the prophets of Baal, stopped and started rain. Then a little opposition came and he started moping: *I am all alone, God has abandoned me.* God's response? Not first, *oh no, you are not alone.* First: go do what I told you to do. Second, by the way: there are still 7,000 who have not bowed their knees to Baal. Brothers and sisters, we are part of a family more numerous than the stars of heaven or the grains of sand on all the beaches of the world. We are not alone.

## 4. The Cost of Discipleship (vv.25-35)

Jesus has been speaking about following him as a party — a wedding feast, a great supper. Now he wants to balance that. Because there is a cost.

Jesus requires only one thing from his people: total and complete surrender to him.

Verse 26: *If anyone comes to me and does not hate his father and mother, wife and children, brothers and sisters, yes, and his own life also, he cannot be my disciple.* This is not a command to actually hate the people around us. What it means is this: when unsafe family members, unsafe friends, unsaved neighbors see our allegiance to Christ — our refusal to go along with them in the things of the world — they think, *you hate us because you love Christ.* From the world's perspective, our love for Jesus looks like hatred of them. Nothing comes between us and serving God. Not boyfriend or girlfriend, not work, not sports, not church, not even self.

Verse 27: *Whoever does not bear his cross and come after me cannot be my disciple.* The cross here is not a symbol of suffering. The cross is a symbol of death. To carry your cross is not to say, *I will suffer this for Christ.* It is to carry your death — to die to self, daily, and be filled with Christ.

Jesus wants us to think carefully before we follow him. He does not want anyone deceived. The tower builder sits down and counts the cost before he begins, lest he run out of materials and everyone mock him. The king considers whether his 10,000 men can meet the 20,000 coming against him — or whether he should send a delegation and negotiate peace. So consider: following Jesus means you do not exist anymore. You have died to self. You are a new creature, completely and totally dependent on him.

The proud will not forsake all. And because of that they will not enter the kingdom of God.

Verse 33: *Whoever of you does not forsake all that he has cannot be my disciple.* Salt that has lost its flavor is fit neither for land nor for the dung hill — useless. *He who has ears to hear, let him hear.*

God has called you to follow him with a humble heart. A heart that acknowledges he is master and we are not. A heart that cries out: *Who will deliver me from this body of death?* — and then: *I thank God, through Jesus Christ our Lord. For there is now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus.*
